preamble: The voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to the voiced pharyngeal fricative /ʕ/. This shift involves a significant retraction of the place of articulation from the velum to the pharynx, while maintaining the fricative manner and voicing.

examples.note: This substitution is noted in linguistic studies of Arabic, though some attribute it to misspelling rather than a purely phonetic shift.
This shift is a characteristic feature of Sabaic as spoken in Ethiopia during the first half of the first millennium BCE.

sources: Al-Jubouri, A. M. (2024). Substitution in Arabic: Lisan Al-Arab Dictionary as a Model. Theory and Practice in Language Studies, 14(1).

Robin, C. (1998). In Robin, C. & de Maigret, A. (Eds.), Arabia Antiqua: Early Origins of South Arabian States. Rome: IsMEO. (Cited in: Hatke, G. (2025). Again the Question of Judaism in Ancient Ethiopia. ResearchGate.)
